title: Kerala Parotta

subs.name: half maida and half whole wheat flour (atta) for a slightly healthier parotta
subs.notes: texture will be chewier and less flaky

ingredients.name: oil, plus extra for layering and frying

subs.name: ghee for a richer layering flavour
subs.notes: use ghee for the folding step only, oil for frying

steps.text: Mix maida, salt, sugar and baking soda in a bowl. Make a well in the centre and add 2 tablespoons of oil. Rub the oil into the flour. Gradually pour in the lukewarm water, mixing as you go, until a soft dough comes together — it should be softer than chapati dough. Knead for 8 minutes until the dough is smooth and stretchy. Coat with a little oil, cover and rest for 1 hour.

Divide the rested dough into 8 equal balls. Coat each ball generously with oil. Roll each ball into a very thin, almost translucent round disc on an oiled surface — as thin as you can get it without tearing, roughly 35–40 cm in diameter.

Stretch the disc further by holding an edge and gently pulling with oiled hands. Then fold it into a long rope by pleating it accordion-style. Coil the rope into a tight spiral and tuck the end underneath. You should have a small coiled disc. Press it slightly flat and let it rest 15 minutes.
Roll each coiled disc gently into a 15–18 cm round. The layers created by the coil will be visible inside.

Heat a tawa on medium-high until a drop of water skips across it. Drizzle half a teaspoon of oil on the tawa and place a parotta on it. Cook for 2 minutes until the underside is golden in patches. Drizzle a little more oil at the edges, flip, and cook the other side for 1.5–2 minutes.

Remove the hot parotta and immediately clap it between your palms two or three times to shatter the layers into their characteristic flaky, layered texture. Repeat with remaining parottas. Serve at once with beef fry, egg roast or any chicken curry.

substitution_notes: maida → half maida and half whole wheat flour (atta) for a slightly healthier parotta (texture will be chewier and less flaky); oil → ghee for a richer layering flavour (use ghee for the folding step only, oil for frying)
